title: Provider/Publisher Anonymity Research
body:
Research related to provider/publisher anonymity for logos storage. As defined in [our FURPS on privacy preserving filesharing](https://roadmap.logos.co/storage/furps/privacy-preserving-filesharing), we aim to provide anonymity to both publisher and downloader. Therefore, the goal of this research is to:
- Research the related-works and projects and summarize the different designs, and solutions that address this problem. Starting with the approaches that use mixnets/onion routing. Possibly look into different e.g. private information retrieval (PIR). 
- List possible suitable solutions/options for logos storage, and the possible next steps.
- Creating a draft (raw) RFC for provider/publisher anonymity (possibly over libp2p-mix).

title: Analysis of the file-sharing components and privacy requirements
body:
The aim of this research is to identify the information leakage in the logos file-sharing protocol which require either changing to protocol or enhancing the protocol with privacy-preserving schemes such as mixnet (libp2p-mix), onion routing, PIR, etc. 

The analysis first looks into the components of the file sharing client identifying what information are leaked during the file sharing process (both download and publish). Work-in-progress analysis can be [found here](https://hackmd.io/@codex-storage/SySGcN5N-e). Once we have an understanding of what information is leaked and where in the protocol, then we can work out a minimal first version of file-sharing client with anonymity.

title: Rename `Vault` to `Trezor`
body:
As part of our deployment preparation we came up with the concepts of Vaults. IMHO, this work could be beneficial to the rest of the community, maybe make it even to its own ERC. Because of this possibility I would like to suggest renaming of the Vault concept, as there is already semi-conflict with Tokenized Vaults: https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-4626

If you have different ideas, I am open to them :-)

title: how frequent should the proof requests be, how many times does a node need to send proofs a day and based on what assumptions?

can we keep slot-level assumption and do aggregation and is that sound? not even in the erasure-coding dimensions. is it a sound assumption that we're storing things in slots and many slots can be in one node.
is there a better way to do this than having these two extremes?
can we treat all the slots in a node as one? if one test failed then all slots are failing, does this align w/ node-level failure rather than slot-level failures?
maybe we do need to switch it and go the other direction w/ node-level assumptions
is the slot model and assumption sound? we need someone to sit down and get numbers on this

body:
High-level 

Giuliano will describe the problem statement end to end and will take steps to see what solutions and what work needs to be done

- Simulate the failure-rate under conditions of bullet 3
- Understand what DHTs allow for range queries that mitigate these issues
- Want to understand the requirements for a DHT for a system like Codex
- Want to locate a dataset by its hash but may be fragmented across many nodes, no one node holds all fragments, can only announce Merkle Root of dataset, what are the implications of doing this? many nodes have a piece of it but have varying availability of the pieces - mitigation via range queries but murky area in DHT land for differing solutions, unclear what works and what doesn't

title: Investigate Sui
body:
Investigate whether [Sui](https://sui.io) is a suitable platform for the Codex marketplace and bandwidth incentives.
Advantages: performance and scaling
Disadvantages: different programming language, no mainnet yet

title: Claim slot before downloading
body:
Investigate a change to the marketplace design whereby a slot can be claimed by a host before it starts downloading data.

Rationale:

In the current [design of the marketplace](https://github.com/status-im/codex-research/blob/ad41558900ff8be91811aa5de355148d8d78404f/design/marketplace.md), a slot can be claimed by a host by offering both collateral and proof of storage. In other words, a host would need to download the data before it's allowed to fill a slot.

This can be problematic. Because hosts are racing to fill a slot, by all downloading the data, they could overwhelm the capacity of the client that is offering the data to the network. Bandwidth incentives and [dispersal](https://github.com/status-im/codex-research/blob/ad41558900ff8be91811aa5de355148d8d78404f/design/marketplace.md#dispersal) could alleviate this, but this is far from certain.

This is why it's useful to investigate an alternative: allow a host to claim a slot before it starts downloading the data. A host can claim a slot by providing collateral. This changes the dynamics quite a bit; hosts are now racing to put down collateral, and it's not immediately clear what should happen when a contract fails. The incentives need to be carefully changed to avoid unwanted behavior.

title: Model the data distribution across nodes
body:
The idea is to model the Codex platform with as much detail as possible so that we can make projections about failure rates, rebuild overheads, the marketplace, attack mitigations, and other important aspects of it. 

A first plan covering some aspects of the model is here: https://hackmd.io/yTRlMInIRi6fG-nk984ULQ

title: Incentives

labels.name: epic
labels.description: Tracks a yearly team epic (only for codex-storage/codex-pm repo)

body:
Incentives are a crucial part of the system and play a big role in shaping interactions in the network. A core intuition that permeates our design is that without properly aligned incentives a network such as Dagger cannot exist without specializing.

Our current assumption around incentives is that we can scope all interactions to be 1 to 1 and thus reduce the complexity of the incentives mechanism. It is important that we validate this assumptions both through literature review and by simulating some of the expected emergent properties.

**Outstanding Questions**:

Validate our core assumptions around simple 1 to 1 interactions

- [ ] #40
- [ ] Will the expected emergent properties materialize?
  - [ ] #42
  - [ ] #41
  - [ ] Are there any others that we can anticipate?
- [ ] Identify and validate workarounds for the zero-entry problem

**Topics of Interest**:

- [ ] Incentives mechanism design
- [ ] Emergent properties in p2p networks
- [ ] Cryptoeconomic models
- [ ] Spam resistance through economic incentives and penalties

**PoC Opportunities**:

There are a lot of opportunities for simulations here

- [ ] Simulate the expected emergent properties
- [ ] Simulate different adversarial conditions that will break our current assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria**

Identify a suitable incentive structure that is capable of supporting all the expected emergent properties:

- [ ] Are incentives properly aligned to support fair interactions?
- [ ] What emergent properties are possible and under which incentive structure?
- [ ] Is there a fix/workaround for the zero-entry problem?
  - [x] Is it a problem?
